title: Stakeholders
summary: Every business sits at the centre of a web of groups that affect it and are affected by it. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 1.4: the definition of a stakeholder; the internal stakeholders (employees, managers, shareholders/owners) and external stakeholders (customers, suppliers, government, the local community, pressure groups and competitors); the specific interest each group holds; how those interests clash to create stakeholder conflict and how businesses manage it; and the precise difference between shareholders and stakeholders. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis — so you can see that a developed applied chain of reasoning, not a list of names, is what earns the marks.
learningObjectives: Define the term 'stakeholder' and distinguish clearly between internal and external stakeholders.

Identify the internal stakeholders (employees, managers, shareholders/owners) and external stakeholders (customers, suppliers, government, local community, pressure groups, competitors) and state the specific interest of each.
Explain how the objectives of different stakeholder groups can clash to create stakeholder conflict, and how a business can manage it.
Distinguish precisely between a shareholder and a stakeholder, and apply the distinction to a specific business.
Develop an applied chain of reasoning that explains WHY two stakeholder interests conflict for a named business.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Everyone with a stake in the game
simpleExplanation.summary: A stakeholder is anyone with an interest in what a business does — the people inside it who run and own it, and the people outside it who buy from it, supply it, regulate it or live next to it. Because each group wants something different, and the business cannot fully satisfy them all at once, managing stakeholders is really about managing competing interests.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a busy restaurant on a Saturday night. The waiters (employees) want fair tips and a manageable rush; the manager wants the tables turned quickly; the owner who put up the money (shareholder) wants a full till; the diners (customers) want good food fast and cheap; the fish supplier wants to be paid on time; the council (government) wants the kitchen to pass its hygiene inspection; the flats upstairs (local community) want the extractor fan turned down. Everyone has a genuine stake in that one evening, but their wishes pull in different directions. Running the restaurant well is largely the art of keeping those competing stakes in balance.
steps: First, identify who the stakeholders are for the specific business, and split them into internal (inside the business) and external (outside it).
Next, for each group, pin down its specific interest — what exactly does it want from this business?
Then, look for where two interests pull against each other. That clash is stakeholder conflict.
Finally, explain WHY the interests conflict and how the business might manage the clash — through compromise, negotiation, prioritisation or communication.

flashcards.back: Any individual or group that has an interest in, or is affected by, the activities and performance of a business. Includes both those inside the business and those outside it.
An individual or group that is part of the business itself and connected to it directly. The three internal stakeholder groups are employees, managers and shareholders/owners.
An individual or group outside the business that is still affected by it or has an interest in it — customers, suppliers, the government, the local community, pressure groups and competitors.

A shareholder OWNS part of the company by holding shares; a stakeholder is anyone with an INTEREST in the business. All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders (employees, customers, suppliers) are not shareholders.
Job security, fair and rising wages, good working conditions, and opportunities for training and promotion.
Job security, high salaries and bonuses, status and the authority to make decisions, and the resources and budget to run their department successfully.
A strong return on their investment — through rising dividends and a rising share price (capital gain) — plus the long-term survival and growth of the business.
High-quality, safe and reliable products at low prices, good service, and honest, ethical treatment.
Regular, large orders, being paid a fair price and on time, and a long-term, reliable trading relationship.
That the business obeys the law (tax, employment, health and safety, environmental), pays its taxes, creates employment and contributes to economic growth.
Local jobs, spending in the local economy, and protection from harm such as pollution, noise and traffic congestion.
That the business changes a specific behaviour they care about — for example reducing its environmental impact, improving animal welfare or ending poor labour practices in its supply chain.
Fair competition, an interest in the rival's pricing, quality and innovation, and (as a stakeholder) an interest in the market conditions the business helps to shape.

A situation where the objectives of two or more stakeholder groups pull in opposite directions, so satisfying one means disappointing another — e.g. shareholders wanting higher dividends versus employees wanting higher wages.

Never use the two words as synonyms. A shareholder is one TYPE of stakeholder (an owner). Writing 'stakeholder' when you mean 'shareholder', or vice versa, changes the meaning of the answer and loses marks.
Managers and shareholders/owners are internal stakeholders, not external. A common error is to file owners as 'external' because they may not work day to day; ownership makes them internal.

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is an INTERNAL stakeholder of a company?
options: A supplier that delivers raw materials
The local community living near the factory
An employee who works on the production line
A pressure group campaigning against the company
quickCheck.answer: An employee who works on the production line

quickCheck.prompt: What is the correct distinction between a shareholder and a stakeholder?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
A shareholder owns part of the company; a stakeholder is anyone with an interest in the business, so all shareholders are stakeholders but not all stakeholders are shareholders
A stakeholder owns shares; a shareholder only has an interest
A shareholder is external and a stakeholder is internal
quickCheck.answer: A shareholder owns part of the company; a stakeholder is anyone with an interest in the business, so all shareholders are stakeholders but not all stakeholders are shareholders

quickCheck.prompt: A supermarket wants to cut costs, while its workers want a pay rise. This is an example of:
options: A stakeholder conflict between shareholders/owners and employees
A conflict between customers and suppliers
An external stakeholder becoming an internal stakeholder
The government acting as a pressure group
quickCheck.answer: A stakeholder conflict between shareholders/owners and employees

quickCheck.prompt: Which of these is the MAIN interest of a supplier as a stakeholder?
options: The lowest possible prices for finished goods
Regular orders and being paid a fair price on time
Higher dividends from the business's profit
Stricter environmental regulation of the business
quickCheck.answer: Regular orders and being paid a fair price on time

quickCheck.prompt: A business is deciding to relocate a factory abroad to cut labour costs. Which stakeholder group is MOST likely to have its interest harmed, creating a conflict with the owners' interest in higher profit?
options: Shareholders, because profit would rise
The existing local community and employees, who would lose jobs and local spending
Competitors, because the business becomes cheaper
The government of the country the factory moves TO
quickCheck.answer: The existing local community and employees, who would lose jobs and local spending

faq.q: What is the difference between a shareholder and a stakeholder?
faq.a: This is the single most tested distinction in 1.4. A shareholder OWNS a share of the company, which makes them an owner. A stakeholder is anyone with an INTEREST or 'stake' in the company's actions. All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders — employees, customers, suppliers, the community — own no shares at all. Use 'shareholder' only when you specifically mean the owners, and 'stakeholder' when you mean the wider set of interested groups.

faq.q: Are managers internal or external stakeholders?
faq.a: Internal. The three internal stakeholder groups are employees, managers and shareholders/owners, because all three are part of the business itself. Managers are sometimes confused for a separate outside group, but they work within the business and have a direct, contractual connection to it, so they are internal.

faq.q: How is a competitor a stakeholder?
faq.a: A competitor is an external stakeholder because it is affected by, and has an interest in, what the business does — its pricing, quality, innovation and marketing all shape the market the competitor operates in. A price cut or a new product by one firm directly affects its rivals, which is why competitors count as stakeholders even though they do not trade with the business.

faq.q: Why can't a business just keep every stakeholder happy?
faq.a: Because a business has finite resources and the groups want different, often opposing things. Money paid out as higher dividends to shareholders is money not available for higher wages to employees or lower prices for customers. A decision that pleases one group frequently disappoints another, so management is constantly balancing and prioritising rather than satisfying everyone at once.

faq.q: How do businesses actually manage stakeholder conflict?
faq.a: There is no single fix; businesses use a mix of approaches. They can negotiate and compromise (e.g. a pay rise smaller than the union asked for but larger than managers first offered), communicate openly to build trust, prioritise the most powerful or important stakeholders, or seek win–win solutions such as retraining employees whose jobs are being automated. The right approach depends on the business, the stakeholders involved and how much each side can influence the outcome.

sections.content: No business stands alone. From the employees who make the products and the customers who buy them, to the suppliers who provide materials, the government that sets the rules and the community that lives beside the factory, every business is surrounded by groups that affect it and are affected by it. These groups are its stakeholders. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 1.4 — what a stakeholder is, the internal and external groups and their specific interests, how those interests clash to create stakeholder conflict and how a business manages it, and the crucial difference between shareholders and stakeholders. Because 1.4 questions reward applied analysis, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a developed applied explanation beats a list of names every time.
What is a stakeholder?

A stakeholder is any individual or group that has an interest in, or is affected by, the activities and performance of a business. The word captures a simple idea: these groups hold a 'stake' in what the business does. Some stakes are financial (a shareholder's investment, a supplier's unpaid invoice), some are personal (an employee's livelihood), and some are social or environmental (a community's clean air). We divide stakeholders into two groups — internal and external — according to whether they are part of the business or sit outside it. That split matters because the source and nature of the interest differ on each side.

items: **Internal stakeholders** are part of the business itself, with a direct, usually contractual, connection to it. There are three groups: employees, managers and shareholders/owners.
**External stakeholders** sit outside the business but are still affected by it or interested in it: customers, suppliers, the government, the local community, pressure groups and competitors.
**The stake can be financial, personal, social or environmental** — a stakeholder does not have to own the business or trade with it to have a genuine interest in what it does.

sections.content: Internal stakeholders and their interests

Internal stakeholders are the people who make up the business. Because they work inside it or own it, their livelihoods and their money are tied directly to how it performs — which also means their interests frequently pull against one another.

items: **Employees** want job security, fair and rising wages, safe and pleasant working conditions, and chances to train and be promoted. Their whole livelihood depends on the business, so they are highly exposed to its decisions.

**Managers** want job security, high salaries and bonuses, status and decision-making authority, and the budget and resources to run their area well. Their interests can diverge from the owners' — for example favouring growth that boosts their status over profit that boosts returns.

**Shareholders / owners** want a strong return on their investment — rising dividends and a rising share price (capital gain) — together with the long-term survival and growth of the business they own.

sections.content: External stakeholders and their interests

External stakeholders are outside the business but are still affected by what it does or have a clear interest in its behaviour. They range from the customers and suppliers the business trades with, to the government and community that host it, to pressure groups and even competitors.

items: **Customers** want high-quality, safe, reliable products at low prices, good service and honest treatment. They can switch to rivals if disappointed, which gives them real influence.
**Suppliers** want regular, sizeable orders, a fair price paid on time, and a stable long-term relationship they can plan around.
**Government** wants the business to obey the law (tax, employment, health and safety, environmental rules), pay its taxes, create jobs and contribute to economic growth.
**Local community** wants local jobs and local spending, and protection from harm such as pollution, noise, traffic and congestion.

**Pressure groups** want the business to change a specific behaviour they campaign on — cutting emissions, improving animal welfare, or removing exploitation from its supply chain — and can apply influence through publicity, protest and lobbying.
**Competitors** are affected by the business's pricing, quality and innovation, which shape the market they share; they have a stake in how the business behaves even though they do not trade with it.

sections.content: When a question asks about a stakeholder, always give the SPECIFIC interest of that group, not a vague 'they want the business to do well'. Say what employees want (job security, higher wages), what suppliers want (regular orders, prompt payment), what the community wants (local jobs, no pollution). Naming the precise interest is what turns an AO1 point into the start of a developed, mark-earning answer.
Shareholders versus stakeholders

This distinction is tested again and again, and mixing the two words up quietly wrecks an answer. A shareholder owns part of the company by holding shares, which makes them an owner and one particular TYPE of stakeholder. A stakeholder is the far broader term: anyone with an interest in the business, whether they own shares or not. So all shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders — employees, customers, suppliers, the community, the government — own no shares at all. The relationship is one of set and subset: shareholders are one group sitting inside the much larger set of stakeholders. Use 'shareholder' only when you specifically mean the owners, and 'stakeholder' when you mean the wider group of interested parties.

items: **Shareholder = owner.** They hold shares, want dividends and a rising share price, and have voting rights at the company.
**Stakeholder = anyone with an interest.** Includes shareholders, but also employees, customers, suppliers, government, community, pressure groups and competitors.
**Set and subset.** All shareholders are stakeholders; most stakeholders are not shareholders. Getting this the wrong way round is a classic penalised error.

sections.content: Stakeholder conflict and how businesses manage it

Because a business has finite resources and each group wants something different, it is usually impossible to satisfy every stakeholder at once. When the objectives of two groups pull in opposite directions, so that meeting one means disappointing another, we have stakeholder conflict. The classic example is money: a pound paid out as higher dividends to shareholders is a pound not available for higher wages to employees or lower prices for customers. Conflict is not a sign of bad management — it is a structural feature of running any business, and the skill lies in managing it.

items: **Shareholders vs employees:** owners want costs cut and profit maximised; employees want higher wages and secure jobs. Cutting the wage bill lifts profit but hurts staff.
**Shareholders vs customers:** owners want high prices and margins; customers want low prices and high quality. Raising prices boosts returns but can lose customers.

**Business vs local community / pressure groups:** owners want low costs and expansion; the community and campaigners want no pollution, noise or job losses. Expanding a plant or offshoring cuts costs but harms the community.

**Suppliers vs customers (via the business):** suppliers want higher prices for materials; customers want lower prices for the finished good — squeezing the business's costs and pricing from both sides.

sections.content: Businesses cannot make conflict vanish, but they can manage it. Common approaches include negotiation and compromise (agreeing a pay rise between what the union demanded and what managers first offered), open communication to build trust and explain difficult decisions, prioritising the most powerful or most important stakeholders when a choice is unavoidable, and searching for win–win solutions — for example retraining employees whose roles are being automated rather than simply making them redundant. Which approach fits depends on the business, the stakeholders involved and how much influence each side can bring to bear.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Using 'shareholder' and 'stakeholder' as synonyms** — a shareholder is an owner and one type of stakeholder; a stakeholder is anyone with an interest. Swapping the words changes the meaning and loses marks.

**Misclassifying internal and external groups** — employees, managers and shareholders/owners are INTERNAL; customers, suppliers, government, community, pressure groups and competitors are EXTERNAL. Putting owners or managers on the external side is a frequent error.

**Giving vague interests** — 'they want the business to succeed' earns little. State the specific interest: employees want job security and higher wages; suppliers want prompt payment and regular orders; the community wants local jobs and no pollution.

**Just naming a conflict without developing it** — writing 'shareholders and employees conflict' identifies the groups (AO1) but does not explain WHY. The marks climb only when you build the chain of reasoning showing how one interest undermines the other.
**Not applying the conflict to the named business** — a generic clash with no reference to the specific firm in the question earns AO1 but forfeits the AO2 application marks.

**Forgetting how conflict is managed** — questions on managing stakeholders need realistic responses (negotiation, compromise, communication, prioritisation, win–win solutions), not the claim that a business can simply please everyone.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 1.4 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding (identifying the stakeholders and the concept of conflict), AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the question, and AO3 rewards developed analysis — the chain of reasoning that explains WHY interests clash. In an analytic/points scheme each valid point earns credit, but on an 'explain' question the marks live in DEVELOPED, APPLIED points, not in bare names. Watch how the marks below attach to a reasoned, business-specific explanation of conflict rather than to a list of stakeholders.

sections.question: Explain two possible areas of conflict between the stakeholders of a named business. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer** (named business: 'BrightBrew', a fast-growing chain of coffee shops owned by a small group of shareholders).

**Conflict 1: Shareholders versus employees over the wage bill.** BrightBrew's shareholders want to maximise their return on investment, so they favour keeping costs — including wages — as low as possible to protect the profit that funds their dividends. BrightBrew's baristas, however, want higher pay and secure hours to match the rising cost of living. These interests conflict directly because wages are one of the coffee chain's largest costs: every extra pound BrightBrew pays its baristas is a pound less available as profit for the shareholders. If the shareholders press for a pay freeze to lift dividends, staff morale and retention fall, which can worsen service in the shops; if the baristas win a large pay rise, the shareholders' returns are squeezed. The same pool of money cannot satisfy both groups, which is precisely why their objectives clash.

**Conflict 2: Owners versus the local community over a new drive-through.** BrightBrew's owners want to expand by opening a drive-through outlet on the edge of a residential area, because more locations mean more sales and higher profit. The local community, however, wants to protect its quality of life and is concerned about the extra traffic, noise and litter a 24-hour drive-through would bring to its streets. These interests conflict because the very feature that makes the site profitable for BrightBrew — high customer throughput on a busy road — is the source of the congestion and disturbance the residents object to. Pleasing the owners by opening the drive-through directly harms the community's interest in a quiet, safe neighbourhood, so the two objectives cannot both be met on the same site.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks** — the command term 'explain' calls for developed, applied points, so each conflict must go beyond naming the groups:

- **AO1 — knowledge:** the engine credits correctly identifying the stakeholder groups involved and the concept of conflict — shareholders/owners, employees and the local community, and the idea that their objectives oppose one another.
- **AO2 — application:** marks are awarded for tying the conflict to BrightBrew specifically — wages as one of the coffee chain's largest costs, the drive-through on the edge of a residential area. A generic 'shareholders and workers disagree' with no named business does not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis (the developed chain of reasoning):** the top marks go to explaining WHY the interests conflict — because the same money cannot fund both dividends and higher wages, and because the throughput that makes the site profitable is exactly what disturbs residents. This 'because… which means…' chain is what lifts each point.
- **What the engine rewards:** a developed, applied explanation of conflict, NOT a list of stakeholders. Naming 'shareholders vs employees' and 'business vs community' with no development is held to the AO1 marks; building each into an applied causal chain unlocks the AO2 and AO3 marks. Two conflicts, each developed and applied, is the structure that reaches full marks on a [6] question.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for identifying the right stakeholders and the concept of conflict, AO2 for applying them to the named business, and AO3 for the developed chain of reasoning that explains WHY the interests clash. So do not just name two pairs of stakeholders: for each conflict, state the two interests, apply them to the specific business, and then explain WHY satisfying one damages the other. The application and the developed 'because' reasoning are where the marks on a conflict question are won.
A second worked example: an applied conflict decision

sections.question: TechAssemble, a profitable electronics manufacturer owned by its shareholders, plans to replace 200 assembly-line workers with robots to cut costs. Analyse the impact of this decision on TWO stakeholder groups. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**Stakeholder 1: Employees (assembly-line workers).** The workers' core interests are job security and a steady income. Automation directly attacks both: 200 of them face redundancy, and even those who remain may see their roles change or feel their jobs are next. The impact is strongly negative — lost livelihoods, falling morale among survivors, and a possible drop in productivity or industrial action as trust breaks down. This is a clear conflict with the shareholders, whose interest in lower costs is the very reason the jobs are being cut.

**Stakeholder 2: Shareholders / owners.** The shareholders' interest is a strong return on their investment. In the short term the decision looks positive for them: replacing 200 wage-earning workers with robots cuts the largest recurring cost, which — if quality holds — raises profit margins and supports higher dividends and a rising share price. However, the impact is not purely positive: the robots require a large upfront investment that reduces cash in the short run, and if the redundancies trigger bad publicity or a consumer backlash, sales could fall and undermine the very returns the shareholders sought. So the benefit to shareholders is real but conditional.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **AO1 (knowledge):** identifies the stakeholder groups (employees; shareholders/owners) and their interests (job security/income; return on investment).
- **AO2 (application):** ties the analysis to TechAssemble — the 200 assembly workers, robots replacing the firm's largest cost, the upfront investment. Points that never mention this business earn no application marks.
- **AO3 (analysis):** develops the impact with reasoning — why automation harms employees (redundancy → lost income and morale → possible action) and why it only conditionally helps shareholders (lower costs → higher margins BUT upfront cost and reputational risk). Showing both that the decision creates a conflict AND that the benefit to one side is qualified is what reaches the top of the mark range.

sections.content: Where this leads

Stakeholders run through the whole course. The conflict between shareholders (owners) and managers you met here reappears as the divorce of ownership from control in business structures and corporate governance. Stakeholder interests drive the study of business objectives, ethics and corporate social responsibility, and pressure-group influence returns in the external environment. Master the habit built in this lesson — identify the stakeholders, state their specific interests, apply them to the business, and explain WHY they conflict — and you have the template that earns marks across every stakeholder-flavoured question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: explain two possible areas of conflict between the stakeholders of a named business, applying and developing each conflict
